name: Belgium
summary: A solid all-rounder with dual citizenship retained both ways (Belgium lifted its ban in 2008; Belgium is on Bangladesh's DNC list), 5-year naturalisation at a modest language bar, KU Leuven's strong AI master's, IT on the shortage list, excellent healthcare and a top-tier passport — but held back by the trilingual (Dutch/French/German) language burden, the OECD's highest labour-tax wedge (~52%, softened by a new expat regime), a powerful Flemish far-right (N-VA largest, Vlaams Belang surging), a short 12-month post-study search year, and Bangladeshi student visas decided out of New Delhi. Derived overall sits marginally above the 60% inclusion line.

job-market.summary: IT/software roles are on the regional shortage lists amid a ~21,000-worker tech gap, with Brussels offering an English-friendly international market (EU/NATO/MNCs) alongside Flanders tech hubs, though salaries trail Canada and Flanders work is Dutch-gated.

pros.text: Software/IT roles sit on Belgium's regional shortage lists (knelpuntberoepen; IT roles added July 2025) with a projected ~21,000-worker tech shortage by 2026 and 27% of firms citing IT/data as critical staffing needs.
AI engineers average ~€87k (Brussels ~€96k); Brussels is an unusually English-friendly hub (EU institutions, NATO, multinationals), with Flanders adding tech/pharma/biotech.

cons.text: Software-engineer base pay (~€42k–74k) trails Canadian/US levels in absolute terms.
Outside Brussels' international employers, Flemish roles commonly require Dutch, narrowing the realistic market for a newly arrived graduate.

links.title: Nucamp — Most in-demand tech jobs in Belgium 2025 (shortage context)

visa-access.summary: There is no Belgian embassy in Bangladesh — students lodge the D-visa at VFS Dhaka but the decision is made by the Belgian Embassy in New Delhi; funds proof is a modest ~€730/month, with no Bangladesh-specific targeting.

pros.text: The D-visa is lodged at VFS Global in Dhaka (no travel required), and proof of funds is a modest ~€730/month — lower than Canada, Germany or Australia.
No Bangladesh-specific visa ban or heightened-scrutiny listing; a Bangladesh embassy operates in Brussels.

cons.text: Belgium has no embassy in Bangladesh, so the student visa decision is made by the Belgian Embassy in New Delhi, adding a routing step and potential delay.
There is no fast-track student scheme; processing is standard and can lengthen in peak season.

links.title: FPS Foreign Affairs — Visa for Belgium if you live in Bangladesh (official)

citizenship.summary: Five years' legal residence leads to nationality by declaration with only a modest national-language and social/economic-integration bar, dual citizenship retained both ways, and a top-tier passport.

pros.text: Dual citizenship works both ways: Belgium fully lifted its ban on 28 April 2008 (no formalities), and Belgium is on Bangladesh's Dual Nationality Certificate list, so a naturalising Bangladeshi keeps BD nationality.

Nationality by declaration is available after 5 years' legal residence with knowledge of one national language (Dutch/French/German, ~A2/B1) plus social and economic participation; the Belgian passport ranks joint 4th on Henley 2026 (~185 destinations).

cons.text: The 2026 reforms tighten language and social-integration proof and raised the application fee from €150 to €1,000.
The alternative 'naturalisation' route proper requires 10 years and is reserved for exceptional cases.

post-study-work.summary: Graduates get a 12-month, non-renewable 'search year' (A card marked job-seeker) to find work or go self-employed — a short but workable runway, available to each graduating spouse.

pros.text: Non-EEA graduates of Belgian institutions can obtain a 12-month 'search year' residence permit (A card, job-seeker) to find a job or start a business, with both graduating spouses each eligible.
Transition to a work permit is eased where the role is on a regional shortage list.

cons.text: The search year is capped at 12 months and is non-renewable — a short window versus Canada's 3-year PGWP or Germany's 18 months.
By the end of the year the graduate must have secured a qualifying job or self-employment to convert to longer-term status.

links.title: IBZ — Search year after completion of studies (official)
KU Leuven — Working in Belgium after graduation (non-EEA)

spouse-family.summary: Family reunification, free schooling and labour-market access for the spouse of a worker exist, but the accompanying spouse of a student faces conditions, and if both spouses study each holds an independent track.

pros.text: The spouse of a worker (single-permit holder) can obtain labour-market access, and if both spouses study each holds their own permit and search-year route.
Children attend school free of charge and are covered by the mutuelle, with family reunification available subject to income and housing.

cons.text: Family reunification for a student principal carries income/housing conditions, and the dependent spouse's work access is not automatic during the study phase.

msc-study.summary: Strong English-taught CS/AI options (KU Leuven's one-year Master of AI, Ghent) with 600+ English programmes and 20 hrs/week student work, at moderate non-EU tuition (~€4k–10k).

pros.text: KU Leuven (top ~60–70 globally) runs a well-regarded one-year Master of Artificial Intelligence; Belgium offers 600+ English-taught programmes, mostly at master's level.
Non-EU tuition is moderate (KU Leuven ~€4,175–5,940; Ghent ~€6,000–10,000), and students may work ~20 hrs/week; a one-year master qualifies for the search year.

cons.text: Tuition is higher than near-free Germany/Austria, and scholarships for non-EU master's students are limited.

links.title: KU Leuven — Master of Artificial Intelligence (official)

pr-pathway.summary: Five years of continuous legal residence leads to EU long-term residence, a clear route eased by shortage-listed work, though regional integration (Flemish inburgering) adds language demands.

pros.text: After 5 years' continuous legal residence one can obtain EU long-term residence, with study and work time building toward it via a clear sequence.
Shortage-listed tech roles comfortably meet the income/qualification bars.

cons.text: Flanders requires civic integration (inburgering, including Dutch), adding a regional language burden on top of federal conditions.
The N-VA-led government signals a more restrictive migration direction, reducing policy predictability.

income-cost.summary: Housing is more affordable than Toronto or London, and a new expat tax regime can lift net pay, but Belgium's labour-tax wedge is the OECD's heaviest, keeping the surplus moderate.

pros.text: Housing is comparatively affordable for a Western European country, and the inbound-taxpayer (expat) regime — 35% cost-of-expatriation allowance, threshold lowered to €70k — can materially raise net pay for qualifying engineers.

cons.text: Belgium has the OECD's highest labour-tax burden (~52%), so without the expat regime the dual-earner surplus is only moderate.
Moderate tuition plus the study-phase living budget leans on the ~€730/month funds reserve.

links.title: Study.eu — Belgium tuition fees & cost of living
EY — Changes to the Belgian expat tax regime (2025)

healthcare.summary: High-quality universal cover via the compulsory mutuelle — non-EU students register if staying over three months and workers are covered from registration, with children co-insured to 25.

pros.text: Registration with a mutuelle (public health-insurance fund) is compulsory for all legal residents; workers are covered on registration and non-EU students enrol once staying beyond three months.
High-quality care with short waits and a reimbursement model; children are co-insured at no cost up to 25 while studying.

cons.text: The reimbursement model carries co-payments, and there is a short registration gap for new students before cover begins.

culture-language.summary: Brussels is genuinely international and English-friendly, but Belgium's trilingual reality (Dutch/French/German) means daily life and regional integration demand a local language, amid a cool far-right climate.

pros.text: Brussels is one of the world's most international cities, where English works widely across institutions and multinational workplaces.
Funded civic-integration programmes (especially Flanders' inburgering) support newcomers, and work-life balance is good.

cons.text: Belgium is trilingual (Dutch/French/German); daily life and regional integration require the local language, and citizenship needs one national language.
Social acceptance of visibly Muslim South-Asian newcomers is mixed amid a strong far-right and the Molenbeek stigma.

safety-law.summary: Among the world's 20 most peaceful countries (GPI 2025: 16th, 1.492) with upper-tier EU rule of law, tempered by Brussels urban crime and a cool climate for Muslim minorities.

pros.text: Global Peace Index 2025: 16th of 163 (score 1.492) — among the world's 20 safest countries — with upper-tier EU rule of law and an active anti-discrimination body (Unia).
Reliable civil justice and strong women's-safety environment.

cons.text: Brussels carries higher urban crime than the national average, and anti-migrant/anti-Muslim sentiment is elevated under a strong far-right.

links.title: Belga News Agency — Belgium among the world's 20 safest countries in 2025 (GPI)
World Justice Project — Rule of Law Index 2025

politics.summary: Institutions are stable but the right dominates — the Flemish nationalist N-VA is the largest federal party (De Wever government) and the far-right Vlaams Belang is surging — pushing a restrictive migration direction.

pros.text: Despite a fragmented federal system, governance is institutionally stable and skilled-migration channels (shortage lists, single permit) remain open.

cons.text: The Flemish nationalist N-VA leads the federal government and the far-right Vlaams Belang is surging (largest in the European Parliament; first far-right Belgian mayor in Ninove, Dec 2024), pushing migration policy in a restrictive direction.
Anti-Islam rhetoric, the regional ritual-slaughter bans and mosque-funding restrictions signal targeted pressure on Muslim communities.

tax.summary: The OECD's heaviest labour-tax wedge (~52%), but a generous inbound-taxpayer (expat) regime and an existing Belgium–Bangladesh tax treaty soften the burden for qualifying engineers.

pros.text: The inbound-taxpayer regime (since 2022) offers a 35% tax-free 'cost-of-expatriation' allowance for ~5+3 years, with the salary threshold lowered to €70,000 — real relief on Belgium's heavy tax.
A Belgium–Bangladesh double-taxation treaty exists within Belgium's 150+ treaty network, easing repatriation.

cons.text: Belgium has the highest labour-tax burden in the OECD (~52%); without the expat regime, take-home pay is heavily compressed.

links.title: PwC — Belgium: foreign tax relief and tax treaties
Deloitte — Belgian expat tax regime changes (2025)

muslim-diaspora.summary: Brussels has dense mosque and halal infrastructure within a ~6–7% Muslim population (≈20% of the capital), and an organised Bangladeshi presence — but the BD community is small and accommodation is uneven (ritual-slaughter banned outside Brussels).

pros.text: Brussels has ~77 mosques and 60+ halal restaurants within a Muslim population of ~6–7% nationally (≈20% of the capital), plus an organised Bangladeshi presence (Brussels embassy, ABSB students' association, Bangladesh Islamic Cultural Center).

cons.text: The Bangladeshi community is small relative to Canada/UK, and accommodation is uneven: ritual (halal) slaughter is banned in Flanders and Wallonia (permitted only in Brussels), and Flemish rules restrict foreign mosque funding amid a strained sentiment climate.

direct-work-route.summary: A genuinely open degree-based route, with federal complexity the main friction. A BD software engineer targets the highly-qualified single permit / EU Blue Card (salary-threshold tracks, no shortage-list or labour-market test): 2026 highly-skilled thresholds are moderate (Brussels ~€44k, Flanders ~€49k, Wallonia ~€53k; Blue Card higher) and attainable in tech. The employer files the single permit and the worker collects a Visa D from abroad (via the Belgian mission/VFS in New Delhi — no Dhaka post). A clean 5+5 ladder: long-term residence at 5 years, Belgian nationality by declaration at 5 years (A2 language + economic participation, easily met by two earners), with dual nationality permitted both ways. Flanders tightened medium-skilled access from 2026, but the degree-holder Blue Card track is unaffected.

pros.text: Degree + qualifying salary opens the highly-qualified single permit / EU Blue Card (no shortage-list or labour-market test); employer-filed and fully offshore with a 90-day decision.
Aligned 5+5 ladder — long-term residence at 5 years, citizenship by declaration at 5 years — with dual nationality permitted (Belgium within Bangladesh's all-Europe DNC scope).

cons.text: Small, emerging BD track record (~5,000 diaspora; no published BD-by-permit data) — a feasible but not well-trodden corridor.
Three-region federal complexity; no Belgian embassy in Dhaka (Visa D via New Delhi); Flanders raised friction for non-Blue-Card roles from 2026.

links.title: Highly-qualified worker, Belgium — EU Immigration Portal (official)
Nationality by declaration (Art. 12bis, 5-year route) — Justice Belgium (official)
Long-term resident status (PR after 5 years) — IBZ/DOFI (official)
